topic: Type System - repr(transparent) for Newtype FFI

turns.content: Hey, I'm working on porting a C++ library to Rust. The original code uses opaque structs with some function pointers; in Rust, should I wrap those types as newtypes and use #[repr(transparent)]? I have something working, but is there a better way?

Good question! Wrapping C++ types via newtypes with `#[repr(transparent)]` is indeed idiomatic in Rust when interfacing with FFI. It guarantees that the Rust struct has exactly the same memory layout as the original type, which simplifies calling into your C++ code and vice versa. This aligns well with your goal of porting existing functionality.
Okay, so if I define a newtype like this:
Yes! Something like:
That's correct, but remember to add `#[repr(transparent)]` so the memory layout remains compatible:

Yes! That way, you can pass `MyTypeWrapper` pointers to C++ and vice versa without any layout surprises. This is especially useful if your C++ code uses function pointer callbacks or raw memory buffers.
I see. But what's the difference between using #[repr(transparent)] and #[repr(C)]?

`#[repr(transparent)]` guarantees that your Rust struct has exactly the same memory layout as its single field, which is perfect for opaque types used in FFI. On the other hand, `#[repr(C)]` adds padding to ensure fields are aligned according to C standards, which can change the size of your struct even if it only contains one field.
So should I always use transparent then?
For single-field structs used in FFI, yes! Using `#[repr(transparent)]` is idiomatic and safe. It avoids unnecessary padding and ensures compatibility with C types.
Okay, but what about pointers like NonNull? Should I also wrap those?
Yes! Wrapping `NonNull<T>` is common when interfacing with C code that expects raw pointers. You can define a newtype around `NonNull<YourType>`, again using `#[repr(transparent)]`:

```rust
#[repr(transparent)]
struct MyPointerTypeWrapper(NonNull<MyType>);
```
And then use that when calling into C code?
Exactly! This ensures safe, zero-cost FFI interactions while keeping Rust ownership semantics intact. The `#[repr(transparent)]` guarantee is crucial here.
Got it. So when should I use `#[repr(C)]` then?

`#[repr(C)]` is useful for structs that represent C-compatible types or unions, especially if you want to ensure alignment and size compatibility across Rust and C codebases. But for single-field opaque wrappers used in FFI, `#[repr(transparent)]` is simpler and more idiomatic.
Alright, thanks! That makes a lot of sense now.
You're welcome! If you have more questions about porting C++ libraries to Rust or anything else, feel free to ask. Happy coding!

topic: Type System - repr(transparent) for Newtype FFI

turns.content: Imagine I'm building a library that needs to export data to C, and I want to wrap an existing struct in a new type so it looks different but takes up the same memory. How can I make sure this new wrapper has the exact same layout as the inner struct without having to manually calculate offsets?

You are describing the core purpose of #[repr(transparent)] which guarantees that the outer type takes up exactly the same space in memory as its single field. When you apply this attribute to a struct containing one field, the compiler ensures the alignment and size match the inner type perfectly, making it safe for FFI boundaries where C code expects specific sizes.
That sounds convenient, but isn't that just what #[repr(C)] does? I thought I had to use repr(C) to get the same layout as my inner struct so I could pass it directly to a C function.

You are mistaken about the equivalence; #[repr(C)] forces a specific memory layout that might not match your inner type if you have padding or different alignment rules, whereas #[repr(transparent)] specifically promises to copy the exact layout of its single field regardless of what that field is. If your inner struct has no padding in C but your Rust version adds some due to alignment, repr(C) would fail while transparent preserves the original size.
Okay, so I can use this for simple newtypes? But what if I need to wrap a pointer or something more complex?

For pointers like NonNull<T>, #[repr(transparent)] works because the size of a pointer is fixed and aligns perfectly, so wrapping it with transparent ensures the wrapper struct has the same layout as just holding that pointer. This allows you to create a safer Rust type that exposes the exact same ABI to C without requiring manual alignment calculations.
Wait, I thought newtypes had to be opaque or something? If they are transparent, aren't they just aliases?

You are correct that it is not an alias in the sense of identity; the types remain distinct so you cannot assign a Transparent struct directly where a non-transparent type is expected. The compiler enforces this distinction even though the memory layout is identical, preventing accidental misuse while still allowing safe FFI marshaling.
So I can't just drop my wrapper and get the inner value? What happens if I try to pass it through a function expecting the inner type?

If you attempt to assign a #[repr(transparent)] struct where a non-transparent type is required, the compiler will reject your code because the types are not considered equal despite sharing layout. You must explicitly convert or wrap/unwrap using the standard conversion mechanisms provided by Rust's type system.
This seems like a lot of boilerplate just to match memory sizes. Is there any performance cost I should be worried about when using this?

There is no runtime performance cost because #[repr(transparent)] only affects the compile-time layout and does not introduce extra indirection or allocation. The compiler generates code that treats the transparent wrapper as if it were the inner type for memory access, so your application runs at full speed.
Great, but what about when I need to pass this through a C function and then get the value back? Do I lose any safety?

You maintain full safety because #[repr(transparent)] ensures the FFI boundary is clean. The C side sees exactly what it expects in terms of size and alignment, so there are no buffer overflows or misaligned accesses. You can safely pass the transparent wrapper to a C function, receive the data, and then unwrap it back into your Rust type without corruption.

topic: Type System - repr(transparent) for Newtype FFI

turns.content: I'm trying to port a C library into Rust and need a safe newtype wrapper around my existing struct. I thought #[repr(C)] on a single-field struct would align perfectly with the original data, but I keep getting layout mismatches when calling back into C code. Here's what I've tried: "struct Wrapper { inner: OriginalStruct }" with #[repr(C)]. It seems like there's an extra padding byte somewhere that doesn't exist in the C version, breaking strict ABI compatibility.

The issue is likely that #[repr(C)] on a multi-field struct still includes padding for alignment of the first field even if it's only one field. For true zero-cost newtypes where you need exact bit-for-bit layout matching without any hidden bytes or potential variance, #[repr(transparent)] guarantees the same memory layout as its inner type. This is specifically designed for FFI safety when wrapping types like std::ptr::NonNull to ensure no extra padding is introduced that wouldn't exist in the original C data structure.

That sounds promising but I'm worried about safety. Can #[repr(transparent)] actually be used with NonNull or other opaque pointers? I want to make sure my wrapper doesn't introduce undefined behavior when passed across the FFI boundary into C code that expects a specific pointer size and alignment.

Yes, #[repr(transparent)] works safely with NonNull because it guarantees the layout is identical to the inner value without adding extra padding bytes. When you define "struct MyNewType { ptr: std::ptr::NonNull<T> }" with #[repr(transparent)], the compiler ensures that the size and alignment of MyNewType are exactly those of std::ptr::NonNull<T>, so passing it through FFI will not corrupt the pointer semantics or memory layout expectations from C.

I see, but I'm still confused about when to use #[repr(C)] versus #[repr(transparent)]. Are they mutually exclusive? What happens if I try to combine them on a struct with multiple fields like "struct Data { header: u32, body: Vec<u8> }"? Will the transparent attribute override the C requirement or cause a compile error?

They are not mutually exclusive but #[repr(transparent)] takes precedence over #[repr(C)] when both appear on the same struct. If you have "struct Data { header: u32, body: Vec<u8> }" with #[repr(transparent)], Rust will ignore the C requirement and instead ensure the layout matches exactly what std::ptr::NonNull<T> or whatever inner type it wraps has. This means you cannot use #[repr(transparent)] on a struct that contains variable-length fields like Vec<u8> because those types do not have a fixed, known memory layout without additional metadata.

Ah, so I need to avoid Vec inside my newtype wrapper entirely? That makes sense for strict FFI. But what if the original C struct actually uses a dynamic array and I really need that behavior? Is there a way to simulate a safe wrapper that mimics the dynamic nature without breaking the ABI?

If you absolutely require dynamic allocation semantics inside your Rust type but must maintain FFI safety, you cannot use #[repr(transparent)] on any struct containing Vec or similar heap-allocated types because those layouts are not fixed. Instead, you should keep the actual data in a separate owned field within your wrapper and only use #[repr(transparent)] for pointer-wrapping layers like NonNull. For example, you can have "struct SafeWrapper { ptr: std::ptr::NonNull<CStruct> }" where CStruct is the exact match to the original struct, while keeping any dynamic buffers in a separate managed field that is never passed through FFI.

Okay, so my strategy would be to split the responsibility. I'll use #[repr(transparent)] only on the pointer wrapper and keep the actual data structure separate? That sounds like it solves the padding problem without losing functionality.

Exactly. You can define "struct PointerWrapper { ptr: std::ptr::NonNull<OriginalCStruct> }" with #[repr(transparent)] to ensure zero-cost wrapping, then store your Vec or other dynamic data in a separate field that is never exposed to FFI calls. This gives you the best of both worlds: strict ABI compatibility for the pointer layer and flexible runtime behavior for internal data.

Perfect, I think I have a clear path now. One last thing before I start coding—what happens if the original C struct itself contains nested types or unions? Does #[repr(transparent)] recursively guarantee that those nested structures also match perfectly?

#[repr(transparent) only guarantees that the immediate wrapper has the same layout as its direct inner type. If your OriginalCStruct contains a union or another complex type, you must ensure that OriginalCStruct itself was correctly laid out for C in the first place. #[repr(transparent)] does not magically fix incorrect C struct definitions; it simply propagates whatever layout exists on the inner type to the wrapper without adding padding.
